coredata.dc:title: Removal of heavy metals from polluted water using magnetic adsorbent based on κ-carrageenan and N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmani, Zeinab
Ghaemy, Mousa
Olad, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel magnetic adsorbent with high stability and fast recovering properties was prepared based on kappa-carrageenan (CG), nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to remove heavy metal ions from water. N-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal decomposition of a mixture of gum tragacanth (GT) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The prepared N-CDs were functionalized with alkyne groups and then grafted on the magnetic κ-carrageenan (MCG) molecules that were contained azide groups using click reaction. The prepared modified magnetic κ-carrageenan (MMCG) had various active functional groups, which were beneficial to the adsorption of metal ions. The MMCG was characterized by FE-SEM, XRD, FTIR, VSM, EDX, BET, TGA instruments. The maximum adsorption capacity for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ was 114.6, 107.6, 99.2, and 94.2 mg g−1, respectively (pH = 6, T = 333 K, dosage = 50 mg, Time = 6 h, initial concentration = 200 mg L−1). The adsorption rates were described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the experimental data were fitted well with the Langmuir isotherm model. The reusability test of the MMCG showed no significant loss of activity (~5–9%) after three cycles of adsorption-desorption tests. The MMCG is a promising material for the removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater. The maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions from real wastewater was ~88–98%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal ions adsorbent
Magnetic modified kappa-carrageenan
N-doped carbon dots
Click reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of azide functionalized magnetic CG (AFMCG)
Synthesis of modified magnetic κ-carrageenan (MMCG) by click reaction
Factors affecting adsorption of heavy metal ions
Effect of metal ion concentration (isotherms) and contact time (kinetics)
Adsorption of heavy metal from industrial wastewater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Technical Services Center laboratories of chemistry faculties in Tabriz and Mazandaran universities for performing the physico-chemical characterization of the adsorbent samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, AFMCG and MMCG, b) TGA curves of Fe3O4, CG, Fe3O4 /CG and MMCG, c) XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and MMCG, d) VSM magnetization curves of Fe3O4 and MMCG, e) FE-SEM images of CG and MMCG, and f) EDX result of MMCG.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of CG/Fe3O4 and MMCG.
Effect of a) adsorbent dosage, b and c) initial metal ion concentration, d) contact time on the removal percentage of MMCG.
(a) Langmuir and (b) Freundlich isotherms plots for metal ions adsorptio on MMCG.
Kinetic models for the adsorption of metal ions of MMCG.
Adsorption/desorption percentages of tested metal ions during three consecutive cycles of MMCG.
Removal of heavy metals from simulated wastewater by the MMCG.
FTIR peaks for neat Fe3O4, azide functionalized magnetic CG (AFMCG), and magnetic modified CG (MMCG).

row.entry: Magnetic copolymer bead (PVBC) modified by click reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concentration of metals in real wastewater and removal percentage by MMCG.

head.ce:title: Removal of heavy metals from polluted water using magnetic adsorbent based on κ-carrageenan and N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Research Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Research Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Composites Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Composites Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Composites Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel magnetic adsorbent with high stability and fast recovering properties was prepared based on kappa-carrageenan (CG), nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), and Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to remove heavy metal ions from water. N-CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal decomposition of a mixture of gum tragacanth (GT) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The prepared N-CDs were functionalized with alkyne groups and then grafted on the magnetic κ-carrageenan (MCG) molecules that were contained azide groups using click reaction. The prepared modified magnetic κ-carrageenan (MMCG) had various active functional groups, which were beneficial to the adsorption of metal ions. The MMCG was characterized by FE-SEM, XRD, FTIR, VSM, EDX, BET, TGA instruments. The maximum adsorption capacity for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ was 114.6, 107.6, 99.2, and 94.2 mg g−1, respectively (pH = 6, T = 333 K, dosage = 50 mg, Time = 6 h, initial concentration = 200 mg L−1). The adsorption rates were described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and the experimental data were fitted well with the Langmuir isotherm model. The reusability test of the MMCG showed no significant loss of activity (~5–9%) after three cycles of adsorption-desorption tests. The MMCG is a promising material for the removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater. The maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions from real wastewater was ~88–98%.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution of water resources has been increased with toxic heavy metal ions due to the advancement of technology, rapid industrialization, and urbanization. The high concentration of heavy metal ions has caused serious health problems for humans and the life of animals and plants (Attallah et al., 2018; Kenawy et al., 2018; Saleh et al., 2017; Saleh et al., 2018; Sarı et al., 2021; Shafiee et al., 2019; Song et al., 2018; Tuzen et al., 2022; Uddin, 2017; Yi et al., 2018). There are several methods for the elimination of heavy metal ions from contaminated aqueous media such as chemical precipitation, solvent extraction, reverse osmosis, ion exchange, filtration and membrane processes, and adsorption (Attallah et al., 2014; Attallah et al., 2016; Demiral and Güngör, 2016; Fakhre and Ibrahim, 2018; Fu and Wang, 2011; Sarı et al., 2021; Tuzen et al., 2020). Among these methods, the adsorption of heavy metals is chosen for its simplicity, effectiveness, cheapness, and reusability. Surface modification of adsorbents leads to changes in properties such as surface energy, surface loading, roughness, surface area, hydrophobicity, reactivity, and functional groups. Many researchers have reported significant improvements to the surfaces of several materials to achieve an influential adsorbent for the elimination of metal ions from aquatic media (Abegunde et al., 2020; Sarı et al., 2021). Polysaccharides are valuable and low-cost materials (Rahmani et al., 2018) that can be chemically modified for more effectiveness in the adsorption of metal ions. Modification of polysaccharides can lead to the fabrication of nanocomposite materials with the desired functional groups to be used as effective adsorbents of metal ions (Banerjee and Chen, 2007; Charpentier et al., 2016; Luo et al., 2016). Also, the application of the magnetic nanoparticles in the matrix of the adsorbent has attracted much attention because of its high surface reactivity and fast recovery from wastewater by using an external magnet (Ge et al., 2018; Lapwanit et al., 2016). One of the low-cost natural polysaccharides, K-carrageenan (CG), is mainly used as a gelling agent in the food industry and as biocatalysts in industrial processes. It is a sulfated polysaccharide with interesting properties such as nontoxic, mucoadhesive, biodegradable, and biocompatible. Modification of natural polysaccharides with the nanoparticles having effective functional groups and high surface-area-to-volume ratio can also lead to the development of adsorbents with a high capacity of pollutant adsorption. While CG is soluble in water, the surface modification increases its stability in an aqueous solution.

In the present work, we intended to synthesize an efficient adsorbent with a large surface area, high physical strength, stability in water, and high adsorption capacity for the removal of toxic heavy metal ions from water. For this aim, κ-carrageenan (CG) polysaccharide was mixed with the Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and then functionalized with azide groups (AFMCG). The prepared AFMCG molecules were grafted with the synthesized N-CDs in DMF\triethylamine\propargyl bromide solution by using a click reaction. The N-CDs and Fe3O4 were used as enhancement agents of the surface reactivity and easy separation. The prepared modified magnetic κ-carrageenan (MMCG) adsorbent was characterized by using various methods such as FTIR, TGA, XRD, VSM, EDX, BET, and FE-SEM. The effect of experimental conditions such as contact time, initial metal ion concentration, adsorbent dosage, and pH value was investigated for the adsorption of Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ ions from aqueous media. The adsorption kinetics, isotherms, reusability of the adsorbent by the sequential adsorption/desorption cycles, and removal performance of adsorbent on the real wastewater were also evaluated.

The κ-carrageenan was purchased from Condinson Co. (Denmark). White twisted ribbons of GT (Astragalus gossypinus) were purchased from a local herbal store in Kurdistan province (Sanandaj) in Iran. After the removal of discolored and defective ribbons, they were ground into a fine powder (particle size <354 μ) and kept in a hermetically sealed jar for further use. All other chemicals including ethylenediamine, DMF, and DMSO were purchased from Merck (Germany) and used without further purification.

Carbon dots doped with nitrogen were synthesized according to our previously reported work (Rahmani and Ghaemy, 2019). Briefly, the homogeneous solution of 1 g GT and the required amount of Ethylenediamine (EDA) in 60 mL distilled water was charged inside a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. After separation of the supernatant solution, the product was obtained by freeze-drying. The resulting N-CDs were dispersed in water as a stock solution (0.5 mg/mL) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (MNPs) were synthesized according to the previous work (Mahdavinia et al., 2014). Briefly, iron salts (5.40 g FeCl3·6H2O and 2.00 g FeCl2·4H2O, Fe3+/Fe2+ = 2) were added in 200 mL distilled and stirred vigorously under nitrogen gas flow at 50 °C. Then, 2 M NH3 solution was added dropwise into the mixture to form the magnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4). The pH of the obtained mixture was adjusted at 10 with ammonia solution and stirred at 50 °C for 60 min. The magnetic nanoparticles were separated by an external super magnet and washed with distilled water to reach the pH ~ 7. The purified magnetic nanoparticles were dried at 45 °C. The CG/Fe3O4 was synthesized using the procedure given in the literature (Mak et al., 2014). The κ-carrageenan and Fe3O4 (weight ratio 1 to 2) were added to a round bottom flask containing a mixture of acetic acid, H2O, and DMF (0.3, 0.4, and 75 mL, respectively) and stirred at 110 °C for 24 h. Then, the resulting material was isolated by an external super magnet and washed with ethyl acetate and acetone and dried in a vacuum oven at 45 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of azide functionalized magnetic CG (AFMCG)
ce:section.ce:para: At first, 0.6 g CG/MNPs, 1 g PPh3, 0.6 g LiCl was added to DMF (20 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 3 h. Then, 4 mL CCl4 (41.3 mmol) was added dropwise at 60 °C. After stirring for 12 h, the solution of NaN3 (1 g NaN3 dissolved in DMSO, 15.4 mmol) was added and heated at 80 °C for 15 h. The flask content was isolated by an external super magnet, washed with ethyl acetate and acetone, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 45 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of modified magnetic κ-carrageenan (MMCG) by click reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs particles were grafted to the surface of κ-carrageenan molecules by using click reaction. The click chemistry was proposed by Sharpless and coworkers (Kolb et al., 2001) provides a powerful tool for the design and synthesis of novel derivatives from synthetic and polysaccharide materials. This reaction generates an orthogonal azide/alkyne ring using CuI catalysis (Meng and Edgar, 2016). MNPs play two important roles; provide high specific surface area and easy separation of the adsorbent from the solution, and their accelerator property leads to the formation of the azido groups into CG main chains (Dave and Chopda, 2014). At first, 0.5 g N-CDs were dissolved in 10 mL DMF in a flask, 0.424 g LiCl was added, and stirred under N2 atmosphere for 30 min. Then, 0.4 mL propargyl bromide (5 mmol) was slowly added to the mixture and stirred for 6 h. Finally, 0.6 g AFMCG, 0.05 g CuI, and 0.8 mL triethylamine were added and stirred at 80 °C for 24 h. The resulting material was separated, washed with ethyl acetate and acetone, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 45 °C.

Flame atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS) (Nova AA 400) and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (PS3520UVDD, Japan) were used for measuring the concentration of metal ions in the solution media. A Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) was used to record the spectra on KBr pellets. The surface morphology of the modified nanoparticles was studied using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Model: Hitachi S4160). A vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Kavir Magnetic Co. Iran) was used to study the magnetic properties of the sample. The specific surface area was measured by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, model: BEL Japan, Inc.) analysis. The thermal stability was measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using the Polymer Laboratory instrument (PL, model: STA 503, Germany) from ambient temperature to 600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) of GBC MMA Instrument having CuK α radiation (λ = 1.5417, 2 θ = 10–80° and step size of 0.02°) was used to study the X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the N-CDs.

Adsorption experiments were performed for Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ metal ions in 50 mL aqueous solutions. The pH of the adsorption medium was adjusted by adding a diluted solution of 0.1 M of HCl or NaOH. The removal percentage was determined by using Eq. (1).
(1)
Removal
%
=
C 0
−
C e
C 0
× 100

C
0 and C
e (mg L−1) is the initial and the equilibrium concentration of the heavy metal ions, respectively.

The adsorption experiments were performed for 5 h by changing the adsorbent dosage (0.025–0.1 g), the metal ions concentration (20–200 mg L−1), and the pH values (2–6). The equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q
e , mg g−1) was determined by using Eq. (2):
(2)
Q e
=
C 0
−
C e
m
× V
where C
0 and C
e (mg L−1) represent the initial and the equilibrium concentration of metal ions, respectively. V (L) and m (g) are the volume of the solution and the weight of the dried adsorbent, respectively. To describe the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent, the classical Langmuir and Freundlich models were applied. The two typical adsorption isotherms can be represented by using Eqs. (3 and 4) (Sahraei et al., 2017).
(3)
Langmuir model :
C e
Q e
=
C e
Q max

1
Q max
K L
(4)
Freundlich model : ln

= ln
K F

1 n
ln
C e
where Q
e is the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg g−1), C
e is the equilibrium metal ion concentration (mg L−1), and Q
max is the maximum adsorption capacity (mg g−1). The two constants K
L (l mg−1) and K
F (mg g−1) are related to the Langmuir adsorption constant and Freundlich adsorption capacity, respectively, and n is the Freundlich heterogeneity adsorption intensity parameter related to the bond distribution.
Kinetics of metal ions adsorption was determined by varying the contact time from 1 to 10 h and using an initial metal ion concentration of 50 mg L−1.

In this study, a 50 mL metal ion solution was used and the average result of three experiments was reported. To investigate and interpret the mechanism of the adsorption process, four kinetic models such as pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, Weber-Morris, and Elovich models were applied and expressed as Eqs. (5 and 6) (Sahraei et al., 2017).
(5)
Pseudo − first − order kinetic model : ln
Q e
−
Q t
= ln
Q e
−
k 1
t
(6)
Pseudo − second − order kinetic model :
t
Q e
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
where Q
e and Q
t show the adsorption capacity (mg g−1) of the adsorbent at the equilibrium and at time t, respectively. k
1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order adsorption rate constant and obtained from ln (Q
e
-Q
t ) vs t, and k 2 (g mg−1 min−1) is the pseudo-second-order adsorption rate constant and obtained from the intercept of the plot of t/Q
t
vs t.

The Weber-Morris and Elovich models are applied to find the mass transfer mechanism and multilayer adsorption (Increasing absorption by increasing adsorption sites), respectively. These models are expressed as Eqs. (7) and (8).
(7)
Q t
=
K w
t
− 1 / 2
+ I
(8)
Q t
=
1 β
ln
αβ
+
1 β
lnt
where Q
t (mg g−1) and K
w (mg g−1 min-1/2) are the adsorption capacity of heavy metal ions at time t and the intraparticle rate constant, respectively. t -1/2(min-1/2) and I (mg g−1) are the square root of time and the value of the intercept, respectively. The value of the intercept is related to the thickness of the boundary layer. The parameters α and β are the initial adsorption rate (mg (g min)−1), and the extent of surface coverage and activation energy for chemisorption (g mg−1) which are obtained from the slope and intercepts of the linear plots of Q
t
versus lnt.
The used conditions for study of effective absorption factors are tabulated in Table 1 .

Sequential adsorption-desorption cycles were carried out to examine the reusability of MMCG as an adsorbent. Desorption of metal ions from the loaded adsorbent was performed at room temperature for 3 h in a 0.1 M HNO3 /EDTA solution as a desorbing agent. Then, the adsorbent was removed by an external magnet and washed several times with deionized water to remove excess acid and the remaining ion. The collected adsorbent was reused in the next adsorption cycle. The metal ion concentration in the aqueous phase was measured by AAS for each adsorption cycle. The desorption efficiency (% D) was determined by using Eq. (9) (Fakhre and Ibrahim, 2018).
(9)
% D =
C des
C 0
× 100
where C
0 (mg L−1) and C
des (mg L−1) are the concentration of metal ions in solution before and after desorption, respectively.

The modification of CG with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and N-CDs can lead to a magnetic adsorbent with the unique properties for the separation of metal ions from water. Fig. S1 illustrates schematically the synthesis of magnetic modified CG. The Fig. 1a and Table 2 show the FTIR spectra of neat Fe3O4, azide functionalized magnetic CG (AFMCG), and magnetic modified CG (MMCG). The azide band disappeared in the spectrum of modified CG. This phenomenon showed that κ-carrageenan modification was performed by click reaction.

Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) curves of Fe3O4, Fe3O4 /CG and MMCG are shown in Fig. 1b. The initial weight loss in the TGA curve of Fe3O4 (up to 200 °C) was a result of to the loss of adsorbed water and also dehydration of the surface OH groups. The further weight loss (up to 300 °C) was related to the oxidation of Fe3O4 to Fe2O3 (Ding et al., 2015). Therefore, the total weight loss (up to 600 °C) was estimated at 7.2%. The TGA curve of CG showed a weight loss in two stages: the first weight loss (8.7 wt%) up to 200 °C was due to a loss of moisture and the second weight loss (36 wt%) up to 300 °C was due to the degradation of CG (Sadeghi, 2012). In Fe3O4/CG, the residual weight (char yield) is 70.82 wt% at 600 °C. This indicates that the presence of Fe3O4 in the sample of Fe3O4/CG increases the thermal stability. The MMCG showed higher thermal stability than Fe3O4/CG, and its residual weight at 600 °C was almost 81.9 wt%. Thus, the prepared MMCG had high thermal stability at high temperatures that showed the successful magnetic modification of CG.

The XRD patterns of MNPs and MMCG are shown in Fig. 1c. The spinal structure of the MNPs was also confirmed by XRD data. The diffraction peaks of MNPs as well as their indices appeared at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.5°, 43°, 53°, 57°, 61° and (220), (311), (4 0 0), (422), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0), respectively (Silva et al., 2013). The amorphous peak that appeared at 2θ =18 for the MMCG sample was related to the N-CDs and CG.

The superparamagnetic property of the magnetic adsorbent was important to ensure its application in wastewater treatment. The measurements of the magnetic field dependence of the magnetization of Fe3O4 and MMCG at 298 K are presented in Fig. 1d. According to the obtained data, the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4 and MMCG were 80 and 60 emu g−1, respectively. The value for MMCG was sufficient for it to be removed from solution by an external magnet. In comparison with the pure MNPs, the lower magnetization of the MMCG was attributed to the coating of MNPs by the nonmagnetic amorphous CG.

The Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) micrographs of MNPs and MMCG samples were utilized to investigate the microstructure and surface morphology of CG after modification. As compared with the smooth and tight surface of CG (Fig. 1e), the morphology of MMCG was distinguished as rough and furry. Therefore, the morphology of the surface confirmed the modification of CG. The EDX of MMCG is shown in Fig. 1f. The EDX spectrum of MMCG shows the presence of N, O, C, S, and Fe in the weight ratios of, 20.18, 34.4, 21.73, 2.33, and 21.37, respectively, confirming the presence of N-CDs and Fe3O4 in the MMCG sample.

Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis of CG/Fe3O4 and MMCG samples was carried out and the gained N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 2 . The improved specific surface area of MMCG (113.33 m2g−1) compared to CG/Fe3O4 (47.14 m2g−1) can lead to higher adsorption capacity of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting adsorption of heavy metal ions

ce:section.ce:para: The MMCG has various chelating sites such as amine, hydroxyl, and sulfate (–OSO3 −) groups that are affected at different pH values and can be proton donors or acceptors. The removal efficiency significantly dependents on the nature of the surface charges and the degree of ionization of the MMCG. To study the effect of the pH value on the adsorbate-adsorbent interactions, sorption studies were investigated in the pH range of 2–6. The precipitation occurred at pH > 6 which could lead to the error of adsorption data. The results in Table 3 showed that raising the initial pH value of the adsorption media increased the removal efficiency of metal ions. The κ-carrageenan-based adsorbents are negatively charged at a pH range of 2–10 (pKa of methane sulfonic acid is ~ −2.5) (Mahdavinia et al., 2016). In the acidic condition, complexion and chelating capacity decreased because of the protonation of the sulfate and amino sites (Gamage and Shahidi, 2007). The results indicated that the removal efficiency of MMCG was higher than the removal efficiency of Fe3O4/CG in 50 mg L−1 solution of metal ions at a solid/liquid ratio 1:1000.

The effect of different amounts of MMCG adsorbent on its removal efficiency was studied. As shown oin Fig. 3a, the removal efficiency increased sharply with the increase in solid/liquid ratio of adsorbent from 500 to 1000 mg L−1. Further increase of the adsorbent up to 2000 mg L−1 did not affect much on the adsorption efficiency. Initially, the surface area and the accessibility of the active adsorption sites increased with increasing the adsorbent dosage, which became saturated with the further increase in the dosage. Therefore, 50 mg MMCG in a 50 mL solution was selected as the optimum amount for further adsorption experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ion concentration (isotherms) and contact time (kinetics)
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion concentration is an important factor in the adsorption capacity of an adsorbent. The removal percentage of the heavy metal ions by the MMCG was evaluated in the concentration range of 20–200 mg L−1 (adsorbent dosage = 1 g L−1 or 0.05 g in a 50 mL solution and at pH 6) and time up to 12 h. As shown in Fig. 3b, the removal percentage decreased with increasing the initial concentration of the metal ions. This decrease was due to the saturation of the adsorbent active sites. The results (in Fig. 3c) also showed that the adsorption capacity increased to a certain value and then started to level off at the higher concentrations. The adsorption capacity for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ was 18.72, 18.32, 17.92, and 17.14 mg g−1, respectively, at the initial concentration of 20 mg L−1. The adsorption capacity increased to 114.6, 107.6, 99.2, and 94.2 mg g−1, respectively, when the initial concentration increased to 200 mg L−1. Therefore, the number of metal ions that transport from the solution to the bulk of the adsorbent increased with increasing the initial concentration. This facilitated the interaction between metal ions and active sites of the adsorbent. To describe the effect of various initial concentrations of metal ions on the adsorption capacity of the MMCG and to find the best equilibrium position in the adsorption process, two isotherm models of the Freundlich and Langmuir were applied with the obtained experimental data. The Langmuir isotherm assumes a monolayer adsorbent with the homogeneous surface and equivalent adsorption sites, and the Freundlich model assumes a multilayer adsorbate with different types of adsorption sites on the heterogeneous surface (Puttamat and Pavarajarn, 2016). The isotherm plots and the extracted data were shown in Fig. 4 and Table 4 , respectively. According to the obtained correlation coefficients (R2), the experimental data of the adsorption process was fitted the Langmuir model well. These results confirmed the monolayer mechanism of the adsorption of the selected metal ions. As listed in Table 4, the maximum adsorption capacity (Qm) calculated from the Langmuir model (Eq. (3)) and the experimental maximum adsorption capacity (Qm, exp) are very close to each other. To determine the equilibrium time for the maximum adsorption, the influence of contact time on the removal efficiency was investigated by immersing 0.05 g of the MMCG in a 50 mL solution containing 50 mg L−1 of one of the selected metal ions. As shown in Fig. 3d, the adsorption of the selected metal ions on MMCG increased rapidly initially and has reached equilibrium after ~5 h. The initially fast adsorption can be due to the high concentration of the metal ions and the presence of many active sites on the surface of the adsorbent. The removal percentage was negligible after 5 h and remained unchanged after 6 h, which can be due to the decrease in the metal ions concentration and also the consumption of the free active adsorptive sites. Due to the occupancy of many active sites, the removal rate decreased gradually and reached the equilibrium value.

The rate of sorption of the selected metal ions was studied by contacting 1.0 g L−1 MMCG in 20 mg L−1 of the pollutant solution for specific time intervals. The effect of contact time on the removal of Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ was shown in Fig. 3d. To investigate the mechanism and the characteristics of adsorption, four kinetic models including pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, Elovich, and Weber−Morris intraparticle diffusion were applied to evaluate the obtained experimental data (Kumar et al., 2010; Lim and Lee, 2015; Masoumi et al., 2014; Sahraei and Ghaemy, 2017; Sahraei et al., 2017). The linear plots of the kinetic models for the tested metal ions were shown in Fig. 5 . The parameters of the kinetic models and the determination of coefficients (R2) were calculated and used to examine the conformity of the models with the experimental data. The highest correlation coefficients (R2) determine the conformity of the experimental data with the kinetic models. As shown in Table 4, the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.994) is much closer to unity for the tested metal ions. As listed in Table 4, the maximum adsorption capacity (Qe,cal) calculated from the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Eq. (6)) and the experimental maximum amount (Qe,exp) were very close to each other. Therefore, these results indicated the suitability of the pseudo-second-order model, and the chemisorption was the rate-controlling step of the sorption process. This suggested that adsorption involved valence forces through the sharing or exchange of electrons between adsorbent and sorbate. The adsorption rate was also affected by the concentration of the metal ions at the adsorbent surface. The maximum adsorption capacity (Qm) of the MMCG was compared with the Qm values of other adsorbents reported in the literature and listed in Table 5 . As can be seen, the as-prepared adsorbent can be employed as a highly efficient adsorbent for the removal of heavy metal ions from aqueous media.

The reusability of the adsorbent is a key factor after each process of the adsorption. Three consecutive adsorption /desorption cycles were conducted in the nitric acid/ EDTA solution under the same conditions for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+ ions. The removal efficiency and desorption ratio were calculated using Eq. (1) and Eq. (9), respectively. and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . The adsorption capacity of the MMCG decreased ~5%, ~5%, 8%, and 9% for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+, respectively, after three cycles of intensive desorption process, while desorption decreased ~5.6, 7.2, 7.5, and 8.4% for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+, respectively, after three cycles. Therefore, it can be claimed that the prepared MMCG has an excellent reusability performance without the loss of much of its activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of heavy metal from industrial wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity of the simulated heavy metal wastewater was investigated in the laboratory with the same amount of the adsorbent and the same contact time (Huang et al., 2020). The maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions was 92.4%, 87%, 64.8%, 40.6% for Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7 . The high adsorption capacity of MMCG for the simulated metal ions solution in the laboratory indicated that it has great potential for treating heavy metals in simulated wastewater.

The maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions by the MMCG from real wastewater (wastewater from a copper plant) was investigated. A 50 mL solution of wastewater was introduced into an Erlenmeyer flask contained 0.05 g MMCG. The mixture agitated on a shaker (90 rpm) for 6 h. The quality of the wastewater was evaluated before and after treatment by determining the concentration of Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ ions. The maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions was shown in Table 6 . The maximum removal efficiency for the simulated and real wastewater indicated that it has great potential for treating heavy metals.

In this study, a novel magnetic adsorbent (MMCG) was prepared with a large surface area, high physical strength, and high adsorption capacity based on the modification of biopolymer K-carrageenan (CG) through grafting of the N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by using the click reaction. The metal ions adsorption of the CG was improved significantly mainly due to the increase of the specific surface area by grafting N-CDs on the CG molecules. The as-prepared adsorbent (MMCG) was used for the removal of Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+ ions from water with the maximum capacity 114.6, 107.6, 99.2, and 94.2 mg g−1, respectively (pH = 6, T = 333 K, dosage = 50 mg, Time = 6 h, initial concentration = 200 mg L−1), and for the synthetic wastewater were 92.4%, 87%, 64.8%, 40.6%, respectively.Also, the maximum removal efficiency of the metal ions from real wastewater was 97.75%, 98.4%, 88% for Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, respectively. Based on the correlation coefficient, the Langmuir isotherm model gave a better fit of experimental data and the adsorption kinetics followed the pseudo-second-order chemical sorption as a rate-controlling step. Regeneration of the adsorbent was carried out using the HNO3/EDTA solution, and the results showed a negligible decrease of ~5%, ~5%, 8%, and 9% for the adsorption capacity of Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+, respectively, and ~ 6.81, 7.3, 9.45, and 9.89% decrease in the desorption after three consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Therefore, the prepared MMCG can be considered a promising adsorbent for the removal of heavy metal ions from polluted wastewater.

Zeinab Rahmani: Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Mousa Ghaemy: Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ali Olad: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Technical Services Center laboratories of chemistry faculties in Tabriz and Mazandaran universities for performing the physico-chemical characterization of the adsorbent samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.hydromet.2022.105915.
